coredata.dc:title: Selective detection of Cu2+ using nitrogen-doped carbon dots derived from humic acid and urea based on specific inner filter effect
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Sheng, Xueying
Li, Shuangying
Zhan, Yali
Guo, Jinghan
Zhou, Boyao
Zhao, Jingyi
Li, Zhi
Liu, Menghua
Li, Yanhui
Qu, Tongxu
Zhou, Qingxiang
coredata.dc:description: A new kind of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) was synthesized via a simple hydrothermal strategy using humic acid as the carbon source and urea as the nitrogen source. The fluorescence intensity of as-prepared N-CDs was quenched significantly in presence of Cu2+ based on a specific inner filter effect, which could be utilized to construct a selective sensor for monitoring Cu2+ in aqueous samples. The sensor exhibited good linearity over the range of 0.1–2 μM, and high sensitivity with a detection limit of 25 nM. Under the optimal conditions, there was no significant interference by other metal ions such as Cd2+, Al3+, Cr3+, Fe3+, Pb2+, Na+, Ni+, Fe2+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Co2+, Mg2+, As3+, K+, Zn2+ for Cu2+ detecting except Hg2+.The interference of Hg2+ can be masked by addition of sodium chloride. The experimental results demonstrated that the prepared N-CDs owned strong fluorescence, high monodispersity, good stability and good water solubility, and the constructed sensor had many advances and great application prospect in environmental field.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots
Cu2+
Fluorescence sensor
Inner filter effect

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Possible fluorescence quenching mechanism of N-CDs sensor
Optimization for the determination of Cu2+
Effect of the concentration of N-CDs
Effect of pH value on the fluorescence quenching

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: N-CDs characterization. (A) The TEM image and (B) UV–vis absorption and Fluorescence spectra of the N-CDs. Curve a is the UV absorption spectrum of 10 μM Cu2+ + 4 mg L−1N-CDs. Curve b and c are the UV absorption spectra of 4 mg L−1N-CDs and 10 μM Cu2+, respectively. Curve d is the theoretical spectrum obtained by adding curve b and curve c. Curve e is fluorescence excitation spectrum of 4 mg L−1N-CDs. Curve f is fluorescence emission spectrum of 4 mg L−1N-CDs. (C) The 3D contour photo of N-CDs. (D) Fluorescence spectra of the N-CDs obtained at different excitation wavelengths. (E) The fluorescence lifetime. Curve g is the fluorescence lifetime of 4 mg L−1N-CDs and curve h is the fluorescence lifetime of 4 mg L−1N-CDs + 10 μM Cu2+. (F) The FTIR spectrum of N-CDs.

Optimization of key parameters. (A) Effect of the concentration of N-CDs, (B) Fluorescence intensity of CDs at different pH, (C) Effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity, and (D) Effect of reaction time on fluorescence intensity.
Linear fitting curve of (F0-F)/F0 values versus different concentration of Cu2+.
(A) Investigation of Interferences of different metal ions (4 mg L−1N-CDs, 6 μM for other cations), (B) Investigation of masking Hg2+ with NaCl over the range of 0–1000 mgL−1.
Comparison of different chemosensors with present developed sensor for the detection of Cu2+.

row.entry: CDs from citric acid and urea
paper-based fluorescent sensor based on radish-derived carbon dots
CDs derived from humic acid and urea

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Spiked recoveries of the four real water samples at three spiked levels by this proposed sensor.

head.ce:title: Selective detection of Cu2+ using nitrogen-doped carbon dots derived from humic acid and urea based on specific inner filter effect

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing, China University of Petroleum-Beijing, Beijing 102249, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing, China University of Petroleum-Beijing, Beijing 102249, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new kind of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) was synthesized via a simple hydrothermal strategy using humic acid as the carbon source and urea as the nitrogen source. The fluorescence intensity of as-prepared N-CDs was quenched significantly in presence of Cu2+ based on a specific inner filter effect, which could be utilized to construct a selective sensor for monitoring Cu2+ in aqueous samples. The sensor exhibited good linearity over the range of 0.1–2 μM, and high sensitivity with a detection limit of 25 nM. Under the optimal conditions, there was no significant interference by other metal ions such as Cd2+, Al3+, Cr3+, Fe3+, Pb2+, Na+, Ni+, Fe2+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Co2+, Mg2+, As3+, K+, Zn2+ for Cu2+ detecting except Hg2+.The interference of Hg2+ can be masked by addition of sodium chloride. The experimental results demonstrated that the prepared N-CDs owned strong fluorescence, high monodispersity, good stability and good water solubility, and the constructed sensor had many advances and great application prospect in environmental field.

ce:section.ce:para: Cu2+ poses a serious threat to environmental protection, human health and animal survival. Cu2+ can cause physiological obstruction, development stagnation or even death when it accumulates to a certain extent whether in human or animal bodies. Long-term exposure to high concentration of Cu2+ can involve liver and kidney, and result in the inactivation of protein which has severe impact on liver and kidney, even leading to intestinal and stomach distress such as vomiting, nausea, and diarrhea, and Wilsons’ disease, meanwhile which is also easily accumulated in the human body and linked to brain diseases such as Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s diseases [1,2]. When it enters into the water, a series of effects would occur on the growth, development, and physiological metabolism process of aquatic organisms. For example, Cu2+ affects the feeding rates of macrobrachium nipponense, alkaline phosphatase, pepsin and trypsin in vivo and is highly toxic to guppies [3,4]. Hence, the admissible level of Cu2+ is 1 mgL−1 in drinking water in China, and World Health Organization (WHO) has set a maximum admissible concentration of Cu2+ at 2.0 mgL−1 for drinking water, and US EPA regulated the maxmium admissible concentration of Cu2+ at 1.3 mgL−1 for drinking water, however adults are permitted to have a daily Cu2+ uptake of 2.0–3.0 mg/day through vegetables according to the documents from FAQ-WHO [5,6]. Therefore, developing effective strategies for highly sensitive and selective detection of Cu2+ is of great importance in various fields.

There are many methods which have been developed for the detection of Cu2+, such as colorimetric method and electrochemical analysis [7,8], electrothermal atomic absorption spectrometry (ETFAAS) [9], flame atomic absorption spectrometry (FAAS) [10], inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) or multi-collector inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [11,12], inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectroscopy (ICP-OES) [13], etc. These methods will provided good sensitivity for the analysis of Cu2+, yet most of these instruments are usually expensive and require proficient operator and rich skills. Biological methods are good alternatives for determination of Cu2+ including enzyme inhibition, immunoassay and biosensor [6,14–16]. Compared with the traditional detection technology, the Biological methods have the advantages of convenience, speed, economy and easy to operate, etc., which is theoretically applicable to the real-time rapid detection of Cu2+. However, the biological reagents should be preserved under severe conditions, and related pretreatment or preparing electrodes were complex.

Recently, fluorescent sensors have absorbed increasing attention from researchers because many fluorescent nanomaterials are introduced into analytical field due to rapid development of science and technology. In a variety of fabrication methods for fluorescent sensors, quantum dots have been used for optical sensing due to their unique optical properties, whereas most traditional quantum dots are based on semiconductors containing heavy metals, such as zinc [17] and cadmium [18], etc. Yet, the toxic effects and potential environmental hazards of quantum dots remain a challenge, which severely limits their practical applications. Compared with heavy metal quantum dots, Carbon dots (CDs), a recently introduced carbonaceous nanomaterial, have the same fluorescence characteristics as quantum dots, and earn the advantages of low toxicity, good stability, convenient preparation and environmental friendliness, which has been a rising star in sensing pollutants, and can be used as excellent alternative to quantum dots. CDs have achieved many applications in biomedical and chemical fields, such as biological imaging, drug delivery, gene delivery, fluorescent probes, food quality and safety assessment, wastewater treatment, photocatalysis [19–24], etc. Carbon dots based sensors have been successfully used to sensing metal ions [25–27]. Our groups have also prepared several CDs with different carbon sources for determination of heavy metal ions with high sensitivity [28–30]. Meanwhile, there are also some applications of CDs for Cu2+ sensing. Hu et al. synthesized N-CDs with medium–low temperature coal tar pitch by chemical oxidation, which was used to establish a robust “on-off-on” switch sensor for Cu2+ with a sensitivity of 0.16 μM [25]. Yang reported a mitochondria-targeted fluorescent sensor based on CDs for Cu2+ imaging in living cells and zebrafish in which CDs were derived from citric acid and 1,2-diaminobenzene by hydrothermal treatment [31]. Praneerad et al. facilely prepared biocompatible radish-derived carbon dots via hydrothermal method and constructed a paper-based fluorescent sensor for Cu2+ determination with a limit of detection of 6.8 μM [32]. Sun et al. fabricated Fe-N-CDs by an electrolysis of carbon cloth electrode coated with monoatomic iron-anchored nitrogen-doped carbon, and the obtained Fe-N-CDs demonstrated good response to Cu2+ with detection limit of 59 nM [33]. Liu et al. prepared fluorescent carbon dots from bamboo leaves and then modified with branched polyethylenimine to achieved branched polyethylenimine capped CDs, which was a sensitive and selective probe for determination of Cu2+ with detection limit of 115 nM [34].

Based on results mentioned above, we can see that CDs derived from different carbon sources earned different properties. In this work, humic acid and urea were selected as raw materials, a new type of N-CDs was synthesized by simple hydrothermal method. It was characterized by UV-spectrophotometer, infrared spectrum and electron microscope, and the quenching mechanism between N-CDs and Cu2+ was described.

Barium chloride dehydrate, calcium nitrate, aluminium nitrate were purchased from Beijing Yili Fine Chemicals Co. Ltd. Calcium chloride anhydrous, manganese chloride, potassium iodide, magnesium nitrate, leadnitrate were purchased from Beijing Chemical Works. Chromium (III) nitrate nonahydrate, chromium trichloride hexahydrate, urea, nickel nitrate hexahydrate were purchased from Aladdin. Iron nitrate nonahydrate was purchased from Beijing Modern Oriental Fine Co. Ltd. Cobalt nitrate hexahydrate, zinc nitrate were purchased from Tianjin Guangfu Technology Development Co. Ltd. Copper nitrate trihydrate was purchased from Xiya Reagent. Ferrous sulphate was purchased from Morden Oriental (Beijing) Technology Development Co. Ltd. Humic acid was purchased from Tianjin Guangfu Fine Chemical Research Institute. Ultrapure water was used throughout the experiments.

UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded by UV-2600 UV–vis spectrophotometer (SDPTOP, Shanghai, China).Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was recorded by a Nicolet Magna-IR 750 spectrometer (Thermo, USA) to substantiate the presence of functional groups. The morphologies of the samples were carried out on JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan).Fluorescence measurements were obtained from an F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan).

The N-CDs were briefly synthesized by using humic acid as carbon source and urea as nitrogen source through a hydrothermal process [35]. 0.2276 g humic acid and 0.1209 g urea were dissolved in 25 mL deionized water. The mixture was stirred evenly under ultrasound for 10 min, and then the solution was transferred to 30 mL teflon autoclave and kept at 180 ℃ for 8 h.The brown turbid solution was obtained. The solution was poured into 50 mL centrifuge tubes and centrifuged at 6500 rpm for 3 min. The supernatant was taken out and the larger particles were removed by filtration with a 0.22 μm filter membrane. N-CDs was purified with dialysis bag (MWCO: 1000 Da) for 24 h and stored at 4 ℃ for use. The N-CDs were dispersed in deionized water at a concentration of 4 mg L−1 for further characterization and use.

10 μL N-CDs solution (4 mg mL−1) was added into a quartz cuvette. Then, NaCl solution (200 mg L−1, pH = 6) and 10 μL different concentrations of Cu2+ were mixed and diluted with ultra-pure water to the final volume of 1.0 mL. After mixing homogeneously and incubating for 2 min, the fluorescence spectrum was recorded immediately at the excitation wavelength of 275 nm. The slot widths of the excitation and emission were both 5 nm. Selectivity is an important feature of the fluorescent sensor, and which was evaluated with 16 metal ions related to organism or environment such as Cd2+, Al3+, Cr3+, Fe3+, Pb2+, Na+, Ni+, Fe2+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Co2+, Mg2+, As3+, K+, Zn2+ and Hg2+.

As shown in Fig. 1
A, the TEM image revealed that the N-CDs in a quadrate shape were homogeneously dispersed. Fig. 1 B shows a weak UV–vis absorption peak at around 280 nm, representing a typical absorption of an aromatic π system (π-π* transition).The broad absorption with a gradual change up to the long wavelength (~600 nm for the N-CDs) indicates the existence of band tail by defect states. At the same time, when the excitation wavelength is 275 nm, the maximum emission peak of N-CDs is 430 nm. As shown in Fig. 1 C and D, when the excitation wavelength increased from 255 nm to 330 nm, the emission peaks of N-CDs basically did not change, indicating that the carbon particle size was relatively uniform and shows the synthesized N-CDs were excitation-independent. The strongest emission at 430 nm was obtained at excitation wavelength of 275 nm. The fluorescence intensity increased with the excitation wavelength from 255 to 275 nm, and then decreased with the excitation wavelength from 275 to 320 nm. When the emission wavelength changes from 200 nm to 430 nm, the fluorescence intensity gradually increases to the extreme value, which may be because of distribution of different surface states and functional groups or various surface energy traps on the N-CDs such as emissive traps, exactions of carbon, triplet carbenes at the zigzag edges, aromatic conjugate structures, and attached functional groups. Fig. 1 E exhibits the fluorescence lifetimes of N-CDs and N-CDs + Cu2+ which were 5.77 ns and 5.70 ns, respectively. FT-IR spectrum presents the functional groups of the N-CDs. Fig. 1 F shows strong absorption bands at around 3423 cm−1 which was attributed to -OH vibration and stretching of -NH bands of phenolic and amine groups. The peaks at 1600 cm−1 are assigned to -NH stretching vibration. The peak at 2028 cm−1 indicates that the N-CDs contain an unsaturated bond C≡C. Moreover, the absorption peaks at 1385 cm−1 and 1105 cm−1 indicated the presence of C-N and O-H. This data suggested that the synthesized N-CDs contained -OH, and -NH groups. Thus, all these characteristic functional groups of N-CDs corroborated the successful synthesis of water soluble N-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible fluorescence quenching mechanism of N-CDs sensor
ce:section.ce:para: From Fig. 1 B, the UV adsorption spectrum of 10 μM Cu2+ + 4 mg L−1N-CDs (curve a) was very similar to that of curve d, with no obvious deviation which meant that there was no complex formation. The absorption of Cu2+ is over the range of 180–240 nm. The excitation spectrum of N-CDs was about in wavelength range of 200–400 nm. The excitation spectrum of N-CDs partially overlapped with the absorption spectrum of Cu2+. Cu2+ may absorb the excitation of N-CDs, resulting in the reduction of fluorescence intensity of N-CDs, which suggested that reduction in fluorescence intensity may be associated with inner filter effect (IFE) or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and photoinduced electron transfter (PET). Generally, fluorescence lifetimes are used to distinguish them. Generally, FRET and PET occur with the apparent change fluorescence lifetimes when the quenchant is added, however fluorescence lifetime is almost not influenced by other chemicals in the inner filter effect [36–38]. In this experiment, the fluorescence lifetimes of N-CDs and N-CDs + Cu2+ were measured (Fig. 1 F). We can see that the average lifetime of N-CDs is almost unchanged in the presence of Cu2+. Therefore, the observed fluorescence quenching should be caused by the IFE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization for the determination of Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: In order to achieve better detection performance of Cu2+, N-CDs concentration, reaction time, pH value and salt concentration were optimized. In the process of optimization, the concentration of Cu2+ was set at 0.6 μM, and the value of (F0-F)/F0 was used as the response signal (F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs solution in the absence and presence of Cu2+, respectively).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of the concentration of N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The concentration of N-CDs in solution is related to the initial fluorescence intensity, which determines the linear range of the development sensor and is the key parameter of the development sensor. The low concentration of N-CDs indicates the low quenching effect and results in narrow linear range. The high concentration of N-CDs will neglect the low quenching from the low concentration of the target analyte. The difference of the signal is almost indistinguishable, and the lower limit may be very high and it is very difficult to sensing low level of the target analyte. The effect of N-CDs concentration on Cu2+ fluorescence quenching was studied. As shown in Fig. 2
A, with the increase of N-CDs concentration, the fluorescence quenching change ΔF increased rapidly before a concentration of 4 mg L−1, and then decreased. Therefore, 4 mg L−1 was used as the optimal concentration for Cu2+ detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH value on the fluorescence quenching
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 2C, the quenching efficiency of Cu2+ on N-CDs increased firstly in the pH range of pH4-6 and then decreased in the pH range of pH6-10. The increase may be due to the increase of fluorescence intensity of N-CDs over pH4-6 (Fig. 2 B). Over the pH range of pH6-10, the quenching efficiency was reduced, which may be due to the fact that Cu2+ was not suitable to dissociate under neutral and alkaline conditions and could form precipitate with hydroxyl ions under alkaline conditions, and the amount of Cu2+ that could react with N-CDs remarkedly declined. According to the experimental results, the optimal quenching performance was achieved at pH 6 (Fig. 2 C). Therefore, pH 6 was used in following experiments.

Longer time will make the chemical sensor lose original advantages, while shorter time will lead to incomplete reaction resulting in reduced fluorescence quenching, narrow linear range and low sensitivity. The optimization was carried out within 0–10 min (Fig. 2 D), it was found that the fluorescence quenching value was stable rapidly without obvious change after the addition of Cu2+. This indicated that N-CDs and Cu2+ reacted quickly. According to the experimental results and for saving time, 2 min was utilized as the optimal reaction time.

In Fig. 3 , with the increase of Cu2+ concentration, the fluorescence quenching of N-CDs increased. The relationship between quenching signal and Cu2+ concentration was shown. The results exhibited that there was a good linear relationship between (F0-F)/F0 and Cu2+ concentration when the concentration of Cu2+ was in the range of 0.1–2 μM with correlation coefficient of 0.99871. The detection limit was calculated as 25 nM. The proposed sensor was compared with the existed ones and the results were shown in Table 1 . Obviously, present sensor earned a good detection limit, which is better than most of the reported sensors. Meanwhile, present sensor was simple, convenient operation and low cost, and had a certain competitiveness for Cu2+ monitoring in water.

The selectivity of N-CDs was evaluated by investigating the effects of coexisted cations such as Cd2+, Al3+, Cr3+, Fe3+, Pb2+, Na+, Ni+, Fe2+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Co2+, Mg2+, As3+, K+, Zn2+ and Hg2+ on the detection performance of Cu2+ with N-CDs, and the results were depicted in Fig. 4 A. In this experiment, Cu2+ was fortified at 0.6 μM and the foreign ion was spiked 10 times higher at 6 μM. From Fig. 4 A, it was clear that the cations except Hg2+ had no significant interference on the fluorescence emission intensity of N-CDs + Cu2+ system. Hg2+ also could quench the fluorescence emission intensity of N-CDs. In order to achieve accurate determination of Cu2+ and resolve the interference of Hg2+, Cl− was considered to form HgCl2 for masking Hg2+. Simply, NaCl was added in the solution, and its concentration was optimized. It was found that the influence of Hg2+ could be eliminated when the concentration of Cl− equal to or over 200 mgL−1 (Fig. 4 B). However, higher concentration of NaCl would led to the decrease of (F0-F)/F0, which meant that the fluorescent emission intensity of N-CDs was restored partially, and the reason was that superfluous Cl− in the solution resulted in the formation of HgCl4 2− and CuCl4 2−. Hence 200 mg L−1 NaCl was employed for further experiments.

In order to verify the reliability and feasibility of the sensor in practical application, and also to verify the applicability of the sensor, four real water samples were taken from Jishuitan Lake, Olympic Forest Park, Binhe Park and Shichahai Lake in Beijing, China. These samples were filtered with microporous membranes and stored for use. Blank samples and fortified samples at three levels of 0.3, 0.6 and 1 μM were analyzed with the developed sensor. The analytical data were listed in Table 2 . In the blank water samples, Cu2+ was not detected. For fortified samples at three spiked levels, the results were satisfied and the spiked recoveries were in the range of 97–109% with relative standard deviation (RSD) lower than 3.46%.

In this work, N-CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal method with humic acid and urea, and which were characterized with FTIR and TEM, etc. FTIR spectrum showed clearly that the obtained N-CDs earned more carbonyl groups and amino groups.The TEM image of N-CDs revealed that the N-CDs in square shape were homogeneously dispersed. Moreover, the N-CDs could be used as an efficient fluorescent probe for the detection of Cu2+ based on IFE. The experimental results demonstrated that the fluorescence sensor had the advantages of simple preparation, high sensitivity, good selectivity and low cost, and has a good detection limit of 25 nM and satisfactory spiked recoveries in the range of 97–109%. The developed sensor showed a broad application prospect.

Xueying Sheng: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Shuangying Li: Software, Visualization. Yali Zhan: Writing - review & editing. Jinghan Guo: Software, Validation, Data curation. Boyao Zhou: Software, Data curation. Jingyi Zhao: Software. Zhi Li: Software. Menghua Liu: . Yanhui Li: . Tongxu Qu: Software. Qingxiang Zhou: Supervision, Software, Data curation, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21976211) and Science Foundation of China University of Petroleum-Beijing (No.2462020XKJS04).
